utterances: When Israel was attacked by Syria and other Arab states in June 1967, the Israeli Army did not initially occupy Ghajar when it seized the Golan Heights because the Israeli maps at the time showed Ghajar in Lebanon, which was not part of the war.
[POST-BROADCAST CORRECTION: We incorrectly stated that Israel had been attacked by Syria and other Arab states in the 1967 war.
In fact, Israel attacked first after Egyptian President Gamal Abdel Nasser expelled United Nations troops from the Sinai and closed the Straits of Tiran to Israeli shipping.]
But by the end of July, Israel had also occupied Ghajar, and the brand-new Israeli maps showed the same division of the village as the earlier Lebanese map.
Since then, Kaufman says, virtually all maps of the region have adopted this division.
This bifurcated view of Ghajar gained further international credence in 2000, when the United Nations drew the Blue Line, the line south of which Israeli troops were to withdraw.
The line went right through Ghajar, and there things remained until 2006, when Israel reoccupied both parts of the village.
The partition of Ghajar, Kaufman argues, is a historical mistake that should be corrected.
Since 2000, the village has been divided.
So there is a human tragedy for these villagers, who are now simply divided between two sovereign states, and they live an impossible life.
There are also political implications.

Analysts say there are many obstacles to a resumption of Israeli-Syrian talks over the Golan Heights; not least the fact that the border region around Ghajar and the much-disputed Shebaa Farms is so murky.
But should peace talks resume, and if Kaufman's findings are correct, the residents of Ghajar can at least hope that someday their village will be reunited.
Peter Kenyon, NPR News, Cairo.
This is MORNING EDITION from NPR News.
I'm Renee Montagne.
And I'm Steve Inskeep.
Hundreds of opponents to Zimbabwe's president held a gathering yesterday.
It's no news that Robert Mugabe faces opposition.
It is news that its opponents got to express themselves.

They were remembering Gift Tandare, an activist who is killed this month in a police crackdown, yet police let his memorial service go ahead, even though it led to new calls for the president to step down.
NPR's Ofeibea Quist-Arcton is in Zimbabwe's capital.
(Soundbite of song ""You'll Never Leave"")
(Singing) You'll never leave.

Young activists gave a stunning rendition of the song ""You'll Never Leave,"" as Gift Tandare's widow and their three children were joined by hundreds of well-wishers, including foreign diplomats, in a church hall here in Harare.
Mr.
YLMAS PURI(ph) (National Constitutional Assembly): I think Tandare is a hero.
Let his blood be our strength.
QUIST-ARCTON: Ylmas Puri of the National Constitutional Assembly, a coalition lobby group, delivered a tribute, so did young Lawrence Mashungu of the Students Christian Movement for Zimbabwe.
Gift, we all know, was murdered in cold blood as he and thousands of peace-loving Zimbabweans tried to exercise their constitutional and God-given right to freedom of worship.
QUIST-ARCTON: Gift Tandare was killed on March 11, after police broke up an opposition rally and detained and allegedly beat up and tortured dozens of opposition leaders and activists.
Speaker after speaker at the memorial, including Mashungu, pledged to continue to fight government repression.
The government of Zimbabwe has failed the people, the economy has crumpled and the democratic space is shrinking each day.
Hallelujah.
(Speaking in foreign language)
(Speaking in foreign language)

Along with the press, came political messages and Zimbabwe's main opposition, Movement for Democratic Change, which has splintered into two factions, was called upon to unite ahead of elections scheduled next year.
President Robert Mugabe has left the Southern African nation since independence 27 years ago.
He has indicated that he wants to remain in power, and he dismisses the opposition as stooges of the West.
His deputy information minister, Bright Matonga, blames the opposition Movement for Democratic Change for ratcheting up tensions.
So they are panicking that Mugabe is coming back again.
He is someone they cannot stand.
The MDC has added even a fourth dimension that they have to remove President Mugabe through violence.
service was Tsvangirai's first public address since the beatings earlier this month, when he was arrested and then hospitalized with a suspected fractured skull and other wounds.
We are not talking of overthrow of government.
Everybody(ph) - we are conscious now of our right to defend democracy in this country.
Listening were diplomats from Australia, Britain, Germany and the U.S.
embassies here in Harare.
They were among the Western countries calling for fresh sanctions against Zimbabwe after its strong-arm tactics against the opposition.
Israeli air strikes have killed more than 370 Lebanese, most of them civilians, and caused widespread damage throughout Lebanon.
More than half a million Lebanese have fled their homes.
But perhaps the biggest difference from 1982 is that today Israel has the support of much of the international community, at least for now.

Although several European leaders have complained that Israel is using disproportionate force, most of the international communities agrees with Israel, that Hezbollah must be pushed back from Israel's border with Lebanon.
Israeli Foreign Minister Tzipi Livni, says Israel has clear goals that the international community must help implement.

In order to dismantle Hezbollah, to take them out of the south part of Lebanon, and to prevent rearmament of Hezbollah in the future - this is a window of opportunity for the international community as for the Lebanese government.
Joseph Alpher, a former senior Israel intelligence official, says that in 1982 Israel was seen as the aggressor in Lebanon.
This time, he says, there is greater sympathy for Israel since Hezbollah sparked the fighting with a cross-border raid, killing eight Israeli soldiers and capturing two others.
Alpher also says the geopolitical situation has changed dramatically since 1982.
We are perceived to be battling the forces of radical Islam, and particularly in the case of Lebanon, Iranian-supported Shiite radical Islam.
And all this is understood by the international community to be connected to the broader fears of Iran's nuclear policies, as well as the broader fears of militant radical Islam in general.
Israeli military officials say they feel they still have a free hand to act against Hezbollah and Lebanon.
They say then need about ten more days to cripple Hezbollah's infrastructure.
But they say unlike 1982, Israel has no intention of staying in Lebanon long-term.
Linda Gradstein, NPR News, Jerusalem.
